title: Commutative and associative properties of addition with integers
description: Rewrite numerical expressions involving subtraction as sums of integers in order to apply the commutative and associative properties of addition.
topic_path: Topic A: Addition and subtraction of integers and rational numbers
Commutative and associative properties of addition with integers

hints:
We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $4$ is the same as adding $-4$.

$7-4+5=7+(-4)+5$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
7-4+5&=7+(-4)+5\\\\
&=7+5+(-4)
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the last two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $7-4+5$:

- $7+(-4)+5$
- $7+5+(-4)$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $-2$ is the same thing as adding $2$, and subtracting $6$ is the same as adding $-6$.

$13-(-2)-6=13+2+(-6)$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
13-(-2)-6&=13+2+(-6)\\\\
&=2+13+(-6)
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the first two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $13-(-2)-6$:

- $13+2+(-6)$
- $2+13+(-6)$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $-9$ is the same thing as adding $9$.

$-23+16-(-9)=-23+16+9$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
-23+16-(-9)&=-23+16+9\\\\
&=-23+9+16
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the last two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $-23+16-(-9)$:

- $-23+16+9$
- $-23+9+16$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $2$ is the same as adding $-2$, and subtracting $-18$ is the same as adding $18$. 

$25+20-2-(-18)=25+20+(-2)+18$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
25+20-2-(-18)&=25+20+(-2)+18\\\\
&=20+18+25+(-2)
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $25+20-2-(-18)$:

- $25+20+(-2)+18$
- $20+18+25+(-2)$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $11$ is the same as adding $-11$ and subtracting $19$ is the same as adding$-19$. 

$27-11+4-19=27+(-11)+4+(-19)$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
27-11+4-19&=27+(-11)+4+(-19)\\\\
&=27+4+(-11)+(-19)
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $27-11+4-19$:

- $27+(-11)+4+(-19)$
- $27+4+(-11)+(-19)$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $22$ is the same as adding $-22$ and subtracting $-21$ is the same as adding $21$. 

$5-22+13-(-21)=5+(-22)+13+21$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the commutative property

The commutative property of addition says that we can add the terms in any order without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
5-22+13-(-21)&=5+(-22)+13+21\\\\
&=21+(-22)+13+5
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we commute the  terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $5-22+13-(-21)$:

- $5+(-22)+13+21$
- $21+(-22)+13+5$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $2$ is the same as adding $-2$.

$-4+5-2=-4+5+(-2)$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
-4+5-2&=-4+5+(-2)\\\\
&=-4+ \Large{(}\normalsize 5+(-2)\Large{)}
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the last two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $-4+5-2$:

- $-4+5+(-2)$
- $-4+\Large{(}\normalsize{5+(-2)}\Large{)}$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $6$ is the same as adding $-6$, and subtracting $15$ is the same as adding $-15$.

$2-6-15=2+(-6)+(-15)$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
2-6-15&=2+(-6)+(-15)\\\\
&=2+\Large{(}\normalsize{(-6)+(-15)}\Large{)}
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the last two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $2-6-15$:

- $2+(-6)+(-15)$
- $2+\Large{(}\normalsize{(-6)+(-15)}\Large{)}$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $-12$ is the same as adding $12$.

$-13-(-12)+2=-13+12+2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
-13-(-12)+2&=-13+12+2\\\\
&=\Large{(}\normalsize{-13+12}\Large{)}\normalsize +2
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the first two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $-13-(-12)+2$:

- $-13+12+2$
- $\Large{(}\normalsize{-13+12}\Large{)}\normalsize +2$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $11$ is the same as adding $-11$, and subtracting $2$ is the same as adding $-2$.

$21-11+8-2=21+(-11)+8+(-2)$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
21-11+8-2&=21+(-11)+8+(-2)\\\\
&=21+\Large{(}\normalsize{-11+8}\Large{)}\normalsize+(-2)
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the terms in the middle.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $21-11+8-2$:

- $21+(-11)+8+(-2)$
- $21+\Large{(}\normalsize{-11+8}\Large{)}\normalsize+(-2)$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $-10$ is the same as adding $10$.

$25+7-(-10)+15=25+7+10+15$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
25+7-(-10)+15&=25+7+10+15\\\\
&=\Large{(}\normalsize25+7\Large{)}\normalsize + 10+15
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the first two terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $25+7-(-10)+15$:

- $25+7+10+15$
- $\Large{(}\normalsize25+7\Large{)}\normalsize + 10+15$

We can rewrite subtraction as adding the opposite of the number.

Once our expression is a sum, we can use properties of addition, like the commutative and associative properties.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting as a sum

Subtracting $27$ is the same as adding $-27$, and subtracting $6$ is the same as adding $-6$. Subtracting $-13$ is the same as adding $13$.

$2-27-6-(-13)=2+(-27)+(-6)+13$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Using the associative property

The associative property of addition says that we can group the terms in different ways without changing the value.

$\begin{align}
2-27-6-(-13)&=2+(-27)+(-6)+13\\\\
&=2+ \Large{(}\normalsize -27+(-6)+13\Large{)}
\end{align}$

The expression is still equivalent when we associate the last three terms.

These two expressions from the choices are equivalent to $2-27-6-(-13)$:

- $2+(-27)+(-6)+13$
- $2+ \Large{(}\normalsize -27+(-6)+13\Large{)}$
